data.title: Commodity Roundup: European natural gas futures rise, gold steady as US jobs data loom
data.description:
European natural gas futures rose above €34/MWh on Thursday, rebounding from an eight-week low of €33, as traders evaluated changing weather patterns and supply conditions.

Although the recent heatwave across Europe is set to ease—likely lowering cooling demand—increased wind power generation in northwest Europe may further reduce gas consumption by power plants.

Global gas markets remain steady as traders assess the impact of heat waves that may boost energy consumption for cooling.

"The hot weather that has gripped Europe is expected to peak in the coming days, triggering red alerts. North Asia LNG prices edged higher amid renewed buying interest from importers," ANZ analysts said.

Elsewhere, gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) held a narrow range, trading around $3,347.81/oz as investors positioned for the U.S. non-farm payrolls data that could influence the Federal Reserve's timeline for interest rate cuts.

Data released by ADP showed U.S. private payrolls dropped by 33,000 jobs in June, marking the first decline in more than two years.

The non-farm payrolls report due at 1230 GMT on Thursday is expected to show an addition of 110,000 jobs in June, down from 139,000 in May, according to a Reuters poll.

Last week, Chair Powell warned that he is still uncertain about the impact of tariffs on consumer price inflation.

"More trade deals at lower tariffs could build some confidence that inflation will remain benign, thus allowing the Fed to ease monetary policy," ANZ analysts said in a note.

Gold typically benefits from lower interest rates as it reduces the opportunity cost of holding the non-yielding asset.

data.title: Google proposes fresh changes to search results to comply with EU's antitrust rules: report
data.description:
Alphabet unit Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) has proposed new changes to its search results in an attempt to fend off increasing criticism from competitors, a week before a meeting that could lead to another EU antitrust fine, Reuters reported citing a document.

In March, the European Commission said Google failed to comply with the EU's Digital Markets Act, or DMA, for two services. The EU watchdog said that Google treated its Google Shopping, Hotels, Flights, "more favorably" in search results versus services offered by third parties and gives "more prominent treatment" to its own offerings.

Google will meet its competitors and the Commission to discuss its proposals during a July 7 to July 8 workshop in Brussels, the report added.

Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Last week, Google offered to create a box at the top of the search page for a vertical search service, or VSS, which would consist of links to specialized search engines and to hotels, airlines, restaurants and transport services, the report noted.

The latest offer, dubbed Option B, is an alternative to last week's proposal, according to a Google document sent by the Commission to involved parties, according to the report.

"Under 'Option B', whenever a VSS box is shown, Google will also show a box that includes free links to suppliers," said the document.

The box for suppliers — in essence hotels, restaurants, airlines and travel services — would be below the VSS box, with Google organizing the information about the suppliers, the report added.

Option B "provides suppliers opportunities while not creating a box that can be characterised as a Google VSS," according to the document.

A Google spokesperson told the news agency that "we've made hundreds of alterations to our products as part of our DMA compliance." 

"While we strive for compliance, we remain genuinely concerned about some of the real world consequences of the DMA, which are leading to worse online products and experiences for Europeans."

Google could face fines if it is found in violation of the DMA.

In September 2023, the European Commission designated for the first time six gatekeepers — Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN), Apple, Meta, Microsoft (MSFT) and Chinese tech giant ByteDance (BDNCE) — under the DMA. Certain products provided by these companies come under the DMA and the EU's Digital Services Act, or DSA — which regulates online intermediaries and platforms that millions of Europeans use every day. The DSA protects consumers and their rights online.

data.title: Zynex says Tricare payment suspension to remain in place
data.description:
Zynex (NASDAQ:ZYXI) on Wednesday said that the company received an update from Tricare that the temporary payment suspension will continue.

"We appealed the temporary suspension and met with Tricare officials in April, where we presented our data and evidence as to why the payment suspension should be lifted," said Thomas Sandgaard, President and CEO of Zynex.

"We received a response from Tricare, and they have decided to continue the temporary suspension while they conduct further review. As such, the temporary payment suspension will continue until Tricare makes its final decision."

Zynex (NASDAQ:ZYXI) fell 3.4% in extended trading on Wednesday.

data.title: What Robinhood's tokenized stocks means for the industry
data.description:
Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) is at it again, looking to transform another part of the financial world. The rise of stock tokenization, and the tokenization of other underlying assets, has sent the company's shares on a blistering rally, climbing 18% over the past three sessions to hit the $100 level for the first time (+163% YTD). It's perfect timing as well, with SEC Chair Paul Atkins hailing tokenization as an "innovation", and vowing to put an end to "regulation through enforcement" by making "clearer rules."

Backdrop: Robinhood's (NASDAQ:HOOD) initial success was the introduction of commission-free trading, which disrupted traditional brokerages and saw nearly all competitors drop their fees to zero. It didn't stop there. The success of fractional share trading and becoming one of the first mainstream brokerages to offer crypto trading saw Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) ride the pandemic-driven trading boom to an IPO in the summer of 2021. The company has since expanded into other financial products like credit cards and banking, prediction markets, and investment advisory, but its latest endeavor is going directly to the rails of the entire industry.

"The long-term arc is building the No. 1 global financial ecosystem," CEO Vlad Tenev said on Bloomberg's Odd Lots podcast. "It involves expanding Robinhood from U.S.-only to fully global and from retail-only to business and institutional. That's where we get into really interesting platform aspects like tokenization."

Why tokens? According to Tenev, this can make it easier to expand equity trading from a 24/5 schedule to 24/7, as well as introduce DeFi aspects like collateralized lending and borrowing, swapping, staking, and self-custody. While Robinhood (HOOD) is using Arbitrum for now, it's planning on building a custom Ethereum-based L2 to power the tokenized stocks, which users across the globe can transact with via stablecoins. Meanwhile, the trading of private company shares is another area that can be accessible to the masses via tokenization. It could even mirror the advertised giveaways Robinhood (HOOD) just ran for tokens of SpaceX and OpenAI, though there has been some drama there following a statement by the latter on X.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TRIP, DDOG, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged slightly higher Thursday morning as investors awaited crucial labor market data for fresh clues on the health of the U.S. economy, while also monitoring the progress of trade negotiations.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Tripadvisor (NASDAQ:TRIP) +6% – Shares climbed after reports surfaced that activist investor Starboard Value has taken a stake of over 9% in the online travel company, valued at roughly $160 million. While Starboard's exact intentions remain unclear, the sizable position signals potential pressure for strategic changes.
  • Datadog (NASDAQ:DDOG) +9% – Shares jumped after it was announced the cloud monitoring firm will join the S&P 500 Index on July 9, replacing Juniper Networks (JNPR) following its acquisition by Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE). The inclusion sparked optimism among investors, with Bank of America also naming Datadog one of its top 10 stock ideas for Q3.
  • Theratechnologies (NASDAQ:THTX) +30% – Shares surged after the biopharma firm agreed to be acquired by CB Biotechnology, an affiliate of Future Pak, for $3.01/share in cash plus a contingent value right (CVR) of up to $1.19/share based on commercial milestones. The deal values THTX at up to $254M, reflecting premiums of 126%–216% over pre-announcement levels. The acquisition follows a strategic review and will add to Future Pak’s portfolio while enhancing patient access and growth.
  • Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) and Cadence (NASDAQ:CDNS) +6% – Shares rallied after the U.S. lifted export restrictions on chip design software to China, resolving a rare earths-related trade dispute. Both companies are restoring access to their electronic design automation tools for Chinese customers, with Synopsys stating systems will be fully updated within three business days. The move marks a key step in easing U.S.-China trade tensions after the licensing requirements imposed in May.

More on related stocks:

  • Datadog: Leveraging AI Capabilities In Core Infrastructure Observability
  • Datadog: No Tariffs On Data, Full Steam Ahead
  • Synopsys: Wait And Watch
  • TripAdvisor stock gains after WSJ report of Starboard taking over 9% stake
  • Synopsys and Cadence cleared to resume chip design software exports to China

data.title: European markets steady as traders assess economic data; US jobs report in focus
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.52%

Composite PMI in the United Kingdom increased to 52 points in June from 50.30 points in May of 2025.

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.23%

France (CAC:IND) +0.02%

The HCOB Italy Services PMI fell to 52.1 in June 2025 from May’s near one-year high of 53.2.

The HCOB Eurozone Composite PMI rose to 50.6 in June 2025.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) rose 0.3% on Thursday, with all sectors and major bourses in positive territory, as investors assessed key regional economic data. 

The HCOB Germany Composite PMI was confirmed at 50.4 in June, marking a return to growth in private sector activity.

Elsewhere, U.S. chip design software firms said they’ve received notices lifting export restrictions to China, signaling that the two countries have resolved a rare earths dispute that had threatened a trade agreement finalized in May.

Global market attention will be on the U.S. as June nonfarm payrolls data is released later in the U.S. trading session.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down 4 basis points at 4.26%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 3 basis points at 2.59%.

The UK's 10-year yield was down 9 basis points at 4.53%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Pioneer AI Foundry launches $2B debt offering via issuance of notes
Alpha and Omega to pay $4.25M to settle with US over export control practices
data.description:
Alpha and Omega Semiconductor (NASDAQ:AOSL) agreed to pay $4.25M to settle with the U.S. Department of Commerce for allegedly shipping items to China's Huawei Technologies in violation of export rules.

Alpha said it reached a resolution with the department's Bureau of Industry and Security, or BIS, to close its investigation of the company's export control practices.

The company noted that the resolution does not impact its ongoing business operations and brings to an end the U.S. government’s five-year-plus investigation, which resulted in no criminal charges.

Alpha engaged in prohibited conduct by forwarding 1,650 power controllers, smart power stages and related accessories to Huawei without authorization in 2019, Reuters reported, citing a department order. Huawei was added to a restricted U.S. trade list in 2019.

The U.S. Department of Commerce did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Alpha said on Wednesday that it is pleased to bring this matter to a close with only limited administrative export control charges.

data.title: Robinhood ticks lower as OpenAI rejects tokenized equity tokens
data.description:
OpenAI issued a firm statement Wednesday denying any affiliation with the tokenized equity tokens offered by trading platform Robinhood, emphasizing that the tokens do not represent real ownership or equity in the company.

The trading platform began offering tokenized shares of OpenAI and SpaceX (SPACE) to its users in Europe.

“These ‘OpenAI tokens’ are not OpenAI equity,” OpenAI wrote on X. “We did not partner with Robinhood, were not involved in this, and do not endorse it.”

The company said that “any transfer of OpenAI equity requires our approval — we did not approve any transfer,” and warned users to “please be careful.”

Robinhood down 1.5% in premarket trading.

 

 

The news follows Robinhood's (NASDAQ:HOOD) introduction of a suite of new crypto-focused products, from tokenized equities and exchange-traded funds to crypto perpetual futures to crypto staking.

“These tokens give retail investors indirect exposure to private markets, opening up access, and are enabled by Robinhood’s ownership stake in a special purpose vehicle,” a Robinhood spokesperson said in response to the OpenAI post.
